questions.text: I experience nervousness as a subtle signal rather than a full-body alarm.
I feel that my daily habits should be engineered to serve my long-term personal vision.
I notice that I can sit with ambiguity and uncertainty without my nervous system escalating.
I hold the view that suffering places a moral obligation on every person who becomes aware of it, and that obligation is not optional.
I routinely carry extra supplies, snacks, or resources in case someone around me needs them.
I sense when a compliment is actually passive-aggressive.
I find that I am naturally oriented toward building my own life rather than supporting someone else's.
I frequently redefine objectives when I think the current ones are wrong.
I set items down wherever is convenient and tidy up when I feel like it.
I feel that the consistency of my moral attention to suffering is the most reliable evidence I have for my own character.
When I hear unexpected good news, the rush of feeling is so strong I sometimes have to sit down.
I instinctively create a chain of command when I work with others.
I hold the view that composure during emotionally charged situations is what allows me to be a stabilizing presence for others.
I instinctively invite others into my decision-making process.
I rarely notice when someone is holding back tears during a conversation.
I find that I miss the unspoken rules about when it is appropriate to speak and when to stay quiet.
I consistently set long-term goals and build step-by-step plans to reach them.
I keep my passwords organized in a secure, systematic way.
I enter difficult conversations open-ended, letting the dialogue shape what I say.
I pick up on when a person's enthusiasm for a plan is masking their resignation about it.
I believe that the ability to evaluate need without emotional bias is what makes genuinely fair decision-making possible.
I am the person who helps refine and improve someone else's plan of action.
I experience embarrassment as a searing, full-body flush that takes a long time to dissipate.
I feel most confident when I am directing a process from start to finish.
I keep track of favors I owe and make a point of reciprocating promptly.
I find that building something for someone else's use is more motivating than building it for my own.
I give my full attention to what's happening now and prepare for future obligations closer to the time.
I hold the view that the moral claims generated by suffering are among the most legitimate demands that can be placed on a person.
I usually do not register the social significance of small talk.
I feel that maintaining a strong sense of self requires consistently investing in myself.
I pick up on tension in a room before anyone says a word.
I believe that the ability to process accounts of human tragedy without personal emotional impact is an asset in any decision-making role.
I believe my even temperament is one of my most reliable qualities.
I experience disappointment as a full-body event, not just a passing thought.
I have been told that I am a great team player.
I hold the view that the best use of any bonus or raise is to share it.
